metadata.title: Research Adaptive Therapy with Claude: Managing Resistance Instead of Maximizing Kill

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Adaptive therapy deliberately leaving sensitive cells alive so they out-compete the resistant subclone — and Zhang 2017 doubling time-to-progression — completely inverts maximum-tolerated-dose intuition for me. I want to understand the ecology. Help me push in: (1) explain why the AR-V7 resistance machinery carries a metabolic fitness cost that only bites in androgen-replete conditions; (2) walk me through the PSA-guided rule of stopping abiraterone at 50 percent of baseline and restarting at baseline; (3) tell me what still blocks this from being standard of care given no completed phase 3 as of 2026.

props.segment: Research Adaptive Therapy with Claude: Managing

beats.narration_text: Standard oncology says: give the maximum tolerated dose to kill the most cancer cells. Adaptive therapy says: that strategy creates the conditions for resistance. The Zhang 2017 trial doubled time-to-progression. Why isn't this standard of care?

The ecological rationale for adaptive therapy: drug-resistant cancer cells carry a fitness cost without drug pressure — the resistance machinery is metabolically expensive. In an untreated tumor, sensitive cells out-compete resistant cells. Maximum-dose therapy kills all sensitive cells, removing every competitor the resistant subclone has. Adaptive therapy doses to maintain a population of drug-sensitive cells — deliberately leaving some cancer alive — because the sensitive cells suppress resistant cell expansion. The PSA oscillation that results looks like treatment failure; it is deliberately engineered ecological management.
Ask Claude to research the adaptive therapy evidence.

props.command: claude "Research adaptive therapy in metastatic castrate-resistant prostate cancer. Explain the evolutionary rationale (why sensitive cells suppress resistant cells, why maximum-dose therapy removes that suppression). What did the Zhang/Gatenby 2017 Nature Communications trial show for adaptive abiraterone dosing? What are the three main barriers to broader adoption, and in which cancers beyond prostate cancer is adaptive therapy being tested?"

props.code:
# adaptive_therapy_brief.md  --  Claude output

## Zhang 2017 (Nature Communications, Moffitt)
- PSA-guided on/off abiraterone protocol
- N=11 evaluable patients
- Median TTP: 27 months vs. continuous ~16 months
- Protocol: stop when PSA drops to 50% of baseline
         restart when PSA returns to baseline

## Three Barriers to Adoption
1. Real-time monitoring tools for non-PSA cancers
2. Patient/clinician discomfort with deliberate incomplete suppression
3. Fitness cost must be real (AR-V7 prerequisite)

## AR-V7 Fitness Cost
- Most common abiraterone resistance mechanism
- AR-V7 cells grow slower in androgen-replete conditions
- This is the ecological prerequisite for adaptive therapy

## Adaptive Trials Beyond Prostate
- Melanoma: BRAF-MEK inhibitors
- Breast: CDK4/6 inhibitors
- NSCLC: EGFR inhibitors

beats.narration_text: Adaptive therapy brief generated: Zhang 2017 correctly identified from Gatenby and Zhang et al. in Nature Communications. PSA-guided protocol confirmed: abiraterone stopped when PSA drops to 50% of baseline, restarted when PSA returns to baseline — creating planned drug holidays. Time-to-progression data: Zhang cohort median 27 months; historical continuous abiraterone approximately 16 months. Follow-up ENACT trial confirmed approach; no phase 3 trial has completed as of 2026. Fitness cost of resistance: AR-V7 splice variant is metabolically costly — cells with AR-V7 grow slower in androgen-replete conditions. This is the ecological prerequisite.
Ask Claude for the best adaptive therapy candidates.

props.command: claude "Identify the three cancer-context combinations where adaptive therapy is most likely to outperform continuous dosing — with rationale for why each has the ecological prerequisites: documented fitness cost, real-time monitoring, and resistance competition asymmetry."

beats.narration_text: Three best candidate contexts: First, CRPC abiraterone and enzalutamide — AR-V7 resistance has documented fitness cost; PSA provides real-time monitoring at low cost; largest evidence base from Zhang 2017 and ENACT. Second, BRAF-MEK inhibitor melanoma — BRAF-mutant melanoma develops resistance via MAPK reactivation; resistant cells grow slower in BRAF-inhibited conditions; real-time monitoring via ctDNA or imaging. Third, CDK4/6 inhibitor breast cancer — resistance via Rb loss or CDK6 amplification; ER-positive breast cancer slow-growing enough for monitoring windows. Prerequisite in all three: resistant cells must pay a measurable fitness cost without drug — the competition asymmetry is the ecological foundation of the strategy.

Adaptive therapy is a philosophical inversion — managing cancer as an ecosystem rather than trying to eliminate every last cell. The PSA oscillation looks like treatment failure by conventional metrics. It is deliberately engineered ecological management. The paradigm shift required is cultural as much as scientific: a clinician who interprets rising PSA as failure will terminate adaptive therapy precisely when it is working. The Zhang 2017 data is robust. The barrier is not the biology.
That's the Cancer Medicine series — nine reels on the molecular logic of why cancer is hard to kill and harder to cure.
